verses.text: The LORD: come in to Pharaoh. I have hardened his heart and his servants' that I might show these signs — and that you may tell your son and your son's son what I have done in Egypt. You shall know I am the LORD. Guidance: God's signs are meant for the next generation as much as the current one. Suffering you endure faithfully now becomes the story your grandchildren will need.

The LORD: come in to Pharaoh. I have hardened his heart and his servants' that I might show these signs — and that you may tell your son and your son's son what I have done in Egypt. You shall know I am the LORD. Guidance: God's signs are meant for the next generation as much as the current one. Suffering you endure faithfully now becomes the story your grandchildren will need.

Moses and Aaron go to Pharaoh: how long will you refuse to humble yourself before the LORD? If you refuse, tomorrow I will bring locusts; they will eat what the hail left, fill your houses, the like of which your fathers never saw. He turned and went out. Guidance: God names pride as the obstacle. The plagues are not random; they are God's repeated invitation to humble Himself.

If you refuse, tomorrow I will bring locusts; they will eat what the hail left, fill your houses, the like of which your fathers never saw. He turned and went out. Guidance: God names pride as the obstacle. The plagues are not random; they are God's repeated invitation to humble Himself.

If you refuse, tomorrow I will bring locusts; they will eat what the hail left, fill your houses, the like of which your fathers never saw. He turned and went out. Guidance: God names pride as the obstacle. The plagues are not random; they are God's repeated invitation to humble Himself.

Moses and Aaron go to Pharaoh: how long will you refuse to humble yourself before the LORD? If you refuse, tomorrow I will bring locusts; they will eat what the hail left, fill your houses, the like of which your fathers never saw. He turned and went out. Guidance: God names pride as the obstacle. The plagues are not random; they are God's repeated invitation to humble Himself.

Pharaoh's servants: how long shall this man be a snare to us? Let the men go, serve their God. Do you not yet realize that Egypt is destroyed? Guidance: Sometimes the people closest to power see clearly before the one in power does. Listen when those around you start naming the cost.

Moses and Aaron are brought back. Go, says Pharaoh — but who will go? Moses: all of us — young and old, sons, daughters, flocks, herds — we must hold a feast to the LORD. Pharaoh sneers: just the men. They are driven out. Guidance: The world keeps trying to negotiate which parts of you belong to God. Whole-family worship is non-negotiable in Moses' answer; let it be in yours.

Pharaoh sneers: just the men. They are driven out. Guidance: The world keeps trying to negotiate which parts of you belong to God. Whole-family worship is non-negotiable in Moses' answer; let it be in yours.

The LORD: stretch out your hand over Egypt for the locusts. Moses stretches; the LORD brings an east wind day and night. Morning brings locusts that cover the face of the whole land — they eat every herb and every fruit the hail did not destroy. No green is left, in tree or field, in all Egypt. Guidance: God's judgments make a complete return impossible. What is consumed will not grow back overnight.

Morning brings locusts that cover the face of the whole land — they eat every herb and every fruit the hail did not destroy. No green is left, in tree or field, in all Egypt. Guidance: God's judgments make a complete return impossible. What is consumed will not grow back overnight.

Pharaoh sends quickly for Moses and Aaron: I have sinned against the LORD your God and against you. Now forgive my sin only this once and entreat the LORD that He take away this death from me. Guidance: He admits sin but treats forgiveness like a one-time favor. Repentance that is mostly relief-seeking is something else entirely.

Pharaoh sends quickly for Moses and Aaron: I have sinned against the LORD your God and against you. Now forgive my sin only this once and entreat the LORD that He take away this death from me. Guidance: He admits sin but treats forgiveness like a one-time favor. Repentance that is mostly relief-seeking is something else entirely.

Moses goes out and prays. The LORD turns a mighty west wind that lifts up the locusts and casts them into the Red Sea — not one remains in all Egypt. But the LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart; he did not let the children of Israel go. Guidance: Mercy comes fully — every locust gone — and is met again with refusal. God's generosity does not depend on a guarantee the heart will turn.

But the LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart; he did not let the children of Israel go. Guidance: Mercy comes fully — every locust gone — and is met again with refusal. But the LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart; he did not let the children of Israel go. Guidance: Mercy comes fully — every locust gone — and is met again with refusal. God's generosity does not depend on a guarantee the heart will turn.

The LORD: stretch out your hand toward heaven, that there may be darkness which may be felt. Three days of thick darkness fall on Egypt — no one rises from his place. But all the children of Israel had light in their dwellings. Guidance: Egypt's sun-god Ra is the silent target. While outside the darkness is total, inside Goshen the light keeps shining. God's protection holds even at noon and at midnight.

Pharaoh: go, serve the LORD; only your flocks and herds stay. Moses: you must let us take sacrifices and burnt offerings. Our cattle go with us — not a hoof shall be left behind. We do not know what we will need to serve the LORD until we get there. Guidance: Obedience can't leave its resources at the gate. What God will require of you is not always known in advance; bring it all.

But the LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart, and he would not let them go. Pharaoh: get away — see that you see my face no more; the day you see my face you shall die. Moses: you have spoken well; I will see your face no more. Guidance: Sometimes obedience ends a conversation. The next time they meet, the deliverance will not be negotiated — it will be enacted.
